findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is multiple, sharply circumscribed, purely lytic "punched-out" lesions scattered throughout the axial and proximal appendicular skeleton. These lesions are relatively uniform in size and characteristically lack any marginal sclerosis or reactive bone formation.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include diffuse background osteopenia and prominent endosteal scalloping. There may also be associated pathological fractures, particularly vertebral compression fractures, due to the severely weakened structural integrity of the bone.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of a surrounding sclerotic rim, mixed blastic/sclerotic activity, or an aggressive periosteal reaction. Furthermore, there is typically no significant soft tissue mass unless there is overt cortical breakthrough.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the multiplicity of purely lytic, uniform "punched-out" lesions without surrounding reactive sclerosis.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Multiple myeloma, given the classic "punched-out" or "rain drop" purely lytic lesions without sclerotic margins in a generalized distribution. This plasma cell dyscrasia predominantly affects the red marrow-containing axial skeleton and proximal appendicular bones, frequently presenting with bone pain and pathological fractures in older adults.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Metastases, however these typically present with more variable, irregular sizes and frequently exhibit at least some degree of ill-defined margins or reactive sclerosis. Additionally, while bone metastases are typically 'hot' on a technetium-99m bone scan, myeloma lesions are characteristically 'cold' or show no tracer uptake.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Lymphoma, given that primary or secondary bone lymphoma can present with multiple lytic lesions and missing it would delay targeted chemotherapy. However, lymphoma usually presents with a more permeative, destructive pattern often with a large associated soft tissue mass, rather than discrete punched-out defects.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately stage the full extent of medullary and extramedullary disease and assess for any impending pathological fractures or spinal cord compression.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is vital to evaluate the progression of the lytic lesions and to determine the patient's baseline skeletal integrity prior to any treatment.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a whole-body MRI, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely defining the true burden of intramedullary disease, detecting subtle lesions before cortical destruction occurs, and assessing for any epidural extension or cord compression.
management.mriSequences: I would specify whole-body T1-weighted and STIR sequences, as untreated active myeloma classically demonstrates low T1 signal and hyperintense STIR signal with a 'salt and pepper', multifocal, or diffuse marrow infiltration pattern.
management.nuclearMedicine: An 18F-FDG PET-CT would be highly beneficial to define the extent of metabolically active medullary and extramedullary disease if whole-body MRI is contraindicated or unavailable, noting that standard technetium bone scans are often falsely negative in myeloma.
management.intervention: If the diagnosis is not already established clinically, I would specify an image-guided bone marrow biopsy, typically of the iliac crest or a focal accessible lesion, to obtain a definitive histological and cytogenetic diagnosis.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Haematology-Oncology MDT. My report would clearly document the distribution of lytic lesions, the severity of any vertebral collapse, and the presence of any spinal canal compromise to guide chemotherapy, bisphosphonate treatment, and possible prophylactic surgical fixation.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is multiple, sharply circumscribed, purely lytic "punched-out" lesions scattered throughout the axial and proximal appendicular skeleton. These lesions are relatively uniform in size and characteristically lack any marginal sclerosis or reactive bone formation. Supporting features include diffuse background osteopenia and prominent endosteal scalloping. There may also be associated pathological fractures, particularly vertebral compression fractures, due to the severely weakened structural integrity of the bone. Importantly, there is no evidence of a surrounding sclerotic rim, mixed blastic/sclerotic activity, or an aggressive periosteal reaction. Furthermore, there is typically no significant soft tissue mass unless there is overt cortical breakthrough. The feature that most influences my thinking is the multiplicity of purely lytic, uniform "punched-out" lesions without surrounding reactive sclerosis.

The appearances are most in keeping with Multiple myeloma, given the classic "punched-out" or "rain drop" purely lytic lesions without sclerotic margins in a generalized distribution. This plasma cell dyscrasia predominantly affects the red marrow-containing axial skeleton and proximal appendicular bones, frequently presenting with bone pain and pathological fractures in older adults. The principle differential would be Metastases, however these typically present with more variable, irregular sizes and frequently exhibit at least some degree of ill-defined margins or reactive sclerosis. Additionally, while bone metastases are typically 'hot' on a technetium-99m bone scan, myeloma lesions are characteristically 'cold' or show no tracer uptake. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Lymphoma, given that primary or secondary bone lymphoma can present with multiple lytic lesions and missing it would delay targeted chemotherapy. However, lymphoma usually presents with a more permeative, destructive pattern often with a large associated soft tissue mass, rather than discrete punched-out defects.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately stage the full extent of medullary and extramedullary disease and assess for any impending pathological fractures or spinal cord compression. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is vital to evaluate the progression of the lytic lesions and to determine the patient's baseline skeletal integrity prior to any treatment. I would recommend a whole-body MRI, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely defining the true burden of intramedullary disease, detecting subtle lesions before cortical destruction occurs, and assessing for any epidural extension or cord compression. I would specify whole-body T1-weighted and STIR sequences, as untreated active myeloma classically demonstrates low T1 signal and hyperintense STIR signal with a 'salt and pepper', multifocal, or diffuse marrow infiltration pattern. An 18F-FDG PET-CT would be highly beneficial to define the extent of metabolically active medullary and extramedullary disease if whole-body MRI is contraindicated or unavailable, noting that standard technetium bone scans are often falsely negative in myeloma. If the diagnosis is not already established clinically, I would specify an image-guided bone marrow biopsy, typically of the iliac crest or a focal accessible lesion, to obtain a definitive histological and cytogenetic diagnosis. This should go to the Haematology-Oncology MDT. My report would clearly document the distribution of lytic lesions, the severity of any vertebral collapse, and the presence of any spinal canal compromise to guide chemotherapy, bisphosphonate treatment, and possible prophylactic surgical fixation.
